description: Hand-curated synthetic prose memories for the CML fidelity benchmark. All entries are fictional; any resemblance to real infrastructure or people is illustrative. Covers two input styles: (1) fact-dense production-memory style (cfg-*, dec-*, con-*, pref-*, fact-*, warn-*, learn-*, runbook-*), short, minimal filler, reads like a structured note. (2) narrative style (narr-*), longer, rambling, lots of connective tissue, reads like someone dictating what happened. The bench cemelifies each into CML and measures how many of the original atomic facts survive; the two styles together show both ends of the compression range (fact-dense condenses 15-25%, narrative 35-60%).

memories.prose: Production Redis runs on redis-prod-a.internal at port 6380 with TLS enabled. Maxmemory is set to 4GB with allkeys-lru eviction. Replica is redis-prod-b.internal, async replication, lag usually under 50ms. Backups hit an offsite object store nightly at 03:15 UTC. We do not persist to disk on the primary; RDB is disabled and AOF is off intentionally so write latency stays under 1ms at p99.

The Postgres cluster is named db-prod-01 and runs version 16.2 on Ubuntu 24.04. Data directory is /var/lib/postgresql/16/main, WAL archiving is on, and archive_command ships to a NAS mount at /mnt/backup/pg-wal/. shared_buffers is 8GB, effective_cache_size is 24GB. Connection limit is 200, with pgbouncer fronting on port 6432 in transaction pooling mode.

We picked sqlite-vec over pgvector for Mnemos because the memory store is embedded, single-writer, and we wanted zero-deployment vector search for the default configuration. Qdrant remains available as an optional backend for users who need multi-node or higher QPS. Decision was made on 2026-02-13 during v6 planning; alternatives considered were pgvector, Chroma, and Weaviate, all rejected because they require a separate server process.

Switched the embedding model from Ollama Qwen3 to FastEmbed multilingual-e5-large on 2026-02-15 (v7.0). Qwen3 gave slightly higher raw cosine on English but was 4x slower on CPU and did not handle Swedish well, which mattered for the production memory store. e5-large is 1024-dim, ONNX format, CPU-native, and runs at roughly 120 passages per second on a Ryzen 7 8-core desktop.

Primary ops contact at the data center provider is Alex Johnson, reachable at alex.johnson@dc-provider.example on weekdays between 09:00 and 17:00 CET. Escalation path goes through the ticket system at priority 2 with tag 'ded-rack'. Avoid calling the main support line for issues related to our specific rack (A-12) because they will redirect anyway.

The user prefers a direct, playful tone in casual replies, never uses em dashes in writing projects (use hyphens or parentheses instead), and wants suggested text blocks wrapped in copyable code fences. Direct, proactive style over overly cautious hedging. When the user asks for a review, they want honest opinions, not validation.

Email replies should be concise: target three to five sentences, match a casual tone, and never include automated footers about being generated by an AI. For internal Slack-style messages, one or two sentences is usually enough. Long-form writing is fine for documentation and RFCs, but not for routine correspondence.

The Ethereum wallet address used for project-related transactions is 0x0000111122223333444455556666777788889999 (illustrative, not a real address). This is cold storage on a hardware wallet, firmware version 2.1.0, with the seed phrase stored in a steel backup capsule offsite. Never share this address outside the project context; there is a separate hot wallet for personal trading on a different hardware device.

The example-shop.com production database lives on db-prod-01 at MariaDB socket /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock, database name prod_shop, user shop_prod_rhclh7. Staging is test_shop on the same server. Binlog format is ROW, retention 7 days, size cap 50GB before oldest rotate.

Do not run db:migrate:fresh against production under any circumstances. The staging box test_shop has a trigger called prevent_fresh that raises an exception, but production does not. We lost 12 hours of customer messages in an earlier incident when that command ran with the wrong --env flag. Always double-check APP_ENV before any migrate command.

The Jina rerank-v2 cross-encoder is trained on query-document pairs and returns raw logits, not calibrated probabilities. To turn the score into something usable as a threshold we apply sigmoid first. Empirically, sigmoid(raw) >= 0.55 correlates well with 'this is actually a duplicate'; anything below is noise. This threshold is set as DEDUP_RERANK_THRESHOLD in mnemos.constants.

Topic-level merge clusters (cosine 0.75 to 0.88) need more aggressive guard rails than tight near-duplicate clusters (above 0.88) because the LLM tends to over-generalize and drop specifics when the inputs are only loosely related. The hierarchical binary merge introduced in v10 mitigates this by only ever asking the LLM to merge two memories at a time, so the 'output roughly one input size' intuition holds.

To rotate the qdrant API key: 1) generate new key with openssl rand -hex 32, 2) write it to /etc/qdrant/config.yaml under service.api_key, 3) systemctl restart qdrant, 4) update MNEMOS_QDRANT_API_KEY in /etc/mnemos/env, 5) systemctl restart mnemos-mcp, 6) verify with curl -H 'api-key: NEW' http://localhost:6333/collections. Old key remains valid until rotation completes.

The Mnemos LongMemEval benchmark reports R@5 of 98.94 percent on hybrid+rerank without CML and 99.15 percent with CML, against the 470 non-abstention questions in the cleaned longmemeval_s.json from the HuggingFace dataset. End-to-end QA accuracy with Sonnet answerer and Opus judge is 77.4 percent on the full 500-question set. All numbers are reproducible from the scripts under benchmarks/.

FastEmbed loads e5-large in roughly 9 seconds on cold start and uses about 2.1GB of RAM. Inference throughput is 120 passages per second on a Ryzen 7 8-core desktop with ONNX CPU runtime. Warm-up happens at MCP server start; the first memory_search after server boot takes about 15ms because the model is already in memory.

So I was trying to get the Stripe webhook integration working yesterday, and honestly it took me a while to figure out what was going on. I kept getting signature verification failures no matter what I tried, and at first I thought maybe the secret was wrong, or maybe the webhook URL was pointing somewhere stale. After probably the third or fourth failed attempt I went back and started reading the Stripe docs more carefully, and it turns out they actually require the raw request body for signature verification, byte for byte, exactly as it came in off the wire. The problem in my case was that my existing FastAPI middleware was already reading the body stream before it ever reached the webhook handler, so by the time Stripe's verification code tried to hash it, the stream was empty or already parsed. The fix, which I only found after another hour of digging, was to disable body parsing on the /webhooks/stripe route specifically, using a dependency override. I am writing this down because I know I will forget.

We spent a good chunk of the afternoon yesterday going back and forth about whether to move the vector store from sqlite-vec over to pgvector, and honestly it went longer than it should have. The main argument for pgvector was that we were already running Postgres for the application data, and it felt a bit silly to also be maintaining a separate embedded vector index. On the other hand, sqlite-vec has been rock solid for us, the deployment story is basically zero (it lives in the same SQLite file as the rest of the memory data), and we do not actually need multi-node vector search at the scale we are running. In the end we decided to stick with sqlite-vec for the default configuration of Mnemos and ship pgvector as an optional backend for people who need it later. The decision was basically driven by the fact that 'zero-deployment' is a feature, not a bug, for the kind of single-user memory system Mnemos is designed to be.

I finally sat down this morning and dug through the actual inference numbers for the embedding pipeline, because I realized I had been quoting different numbers in different places and wanted to get them straight. So here is what I actually measured, on a reference box which is a Ryzen 7 8-core desktop with 64GB of ECC RAM. FastEmbed loading multilingual-e5-large takes about 9 seconds on a cold start, which is annoying but only matters once per MCP server boot. The model itself uses roughly 2.1GB of resident RAM once loaded. Inference throughput, measured with a batch of 100 short passages, averaged out to about 120 passages per second, which is honestly better than I expected for a CPU-native ONNX runtime. The first memory_search after startup finishes in about 15ms, because by then the model is already warm in memory thanks to the warmup hook in the MCP server. I am quoting these numbers more carefully from now on.

I need to write this one down so I never forget it. Back in January, on the twenty-second, I ran db:migrate:fresh against what I thought was the staging database for example-shop.com, and it turned out that the APP_ENV flag was pointing at production. I lost roughly twelve hours of customer messages. The really painful part is that staging actually has a trigger installed, called prevent_fresh, that raises an exception if you try to run a destructive migration, but production does not have that trigger, so production just happily did what I asked. Since then we have a hard rule: never run db:migrate:fresh without first reading out APP_ENV and verifying the target hostname. If I am ever tempted to skip that check because I am tired or in a hurry, this is the memory that is supposed to stop me.

A few quick notes on how I want replies written, because I keep having to repeat this. When the tone is casual, which is most of the time, I prefer a playful direct style over hedged corporate-speak, it just reads more like an actual person talking. No em dashes in any writing project; I find them lazy, and I would rather use hyphens or parentheses or just end the sentence. Suggested text blocks should always be in copyable code fences so I can lift them straight into wherever I am going to use them. Email replies in particular should be concise, target three to five sentences, never more unless there is a specific reason. And when I am asking for a review of something I wrote, I want actual honest opinions, not validation. If the answer to 'what do you think' is 'it is great', I assume you are not telling me what you actually think.
